petitioner_background: The petitioner is an event management professional with experience planning and managing components of large international events, including the Rio 2016 Summer Olympic Games and the 2014 FIFA World Cup held in Brazil. She received a bachelor's degree in social communication from a university in 2000. She sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under INA section 203(b)(1)(A) (EB-1A). She appears to be Brazilian based on her employment with Brazilian local organizing committees and filing of Brazilian tax returns.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of membership in the Sports Marketing Association (SMA), Women in Sports and Events (WISE), and the Worldwide Association of Female Professionals (WAOFP). Supporting documents included copies of the SMA's 'Constitution Bylaws,' a screenshot from the WAOFP website, and an undated letter confirming her WAOFP membership. She also previously claimed membership in the local organizing committee (LOC) for the 2014 FIFA World Cup. She emphasized that she nominated herself for a 'member-at-large' position on the SMA executive board, arguing her qualifications underwent additional scrutiny.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner failed this criterion for multiple reasons. First, the record reflected she was approved for SMA and WISE membership in May 2018, approximately one year after the petition was filed in May 2017, and WAOFP admission date was undocumented. Under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1), eligibility must be established from the time of filing. Second, the SMA's bylaws state professional membership 'is open to any individual who wishes to subscribe to the purposes for which the association is formed,' and the bylaws did not support that an SMA member must demonstrate outstanding achievements for board nomination acceptance. Third, WAOFP evidence stated only that 'members have been vetted and thoroughly interviewed' and 'have demonstrated success in their career pursuits,' which was insufficient. Fourth, no evidence explained WISE membership requirements. The FIFA LOC evidence only demonstrated employment, not committee membership, which the petitioner did not contest.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted one article about her and relating to her work, published by Orlando BrazilUSA Magazine. On appeal, she submitted the magazine's media kit providing information on its online presence, including e-mail subscribers and online views per month. She also provided comparative figures for the Orlando Business Journal, asserting the two publications have a similar reach, and maintained that Orlando BrazilUSA Magazine is 'among major trade publications.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined the article did not satisfy the criterion because the magazine had a print circulation of only 5,000 copies and was not a professional publication, major trade magazine, or other major medium. The AAO further noted that the article was published in 2018, more than one year after the petition was filed in May 2017. Under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1), eligibility must be satisfied from the time of filing through adjudication. The petitioner submitted no evidence of any published materials about her prior to filing, so she did not satisfy the criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence that she prepared materials used by FIFA, the International Olympic Committee (IOC), and the International Paralympic Committee (IPC) to 'transfer knowledge for upcoming sport events and new generations of professionals.' Following the 2014 FIFA World Cup, FIFA hired her to write a handbook chapter for the new LOC in Russia for the 2018 FIFA World Cup. Her materials for the 2016 Olympics and Paralympics were incorporated in a library with more than 30,000 documents available for consultation by sports industry professionals, federations, and students. She described the materials as 'presentations, handbooks, manuals, operational plans.' She also submitted letters from former supervisors, including: a letter from an official of the 2016 Rio Olympic and Paralympic Games Organizing Committee praising her talents, commitment, and contributions; a letter from the Head of a department for FIFA confirming she was engaged as a consultant to write a handbook for the FIFA Club World Cup in Morocco; a letter from a former official for the 2014 FIFA World Cup describing her duties in detail and stating she was 'crucial to the success of the event'; and a letter from a former official of the International Paralympic Committee praising her contributions and critical role.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO acknowledged the letters were detailed but found they only established the petitioner made important contributions to the outcomes of these events—not that her contributions remarkably impacted the field to an extent consistent with 'major significance.' The work was 'original' only in the sense that she created it as part of her job responsibilities. It was unclear how the procedures and guidelines she outlined were novel or different compared to previous editions of the FIFA World Cup and Olympic Games. The fact that her work product was made available in FIFA/IOC libraries was insufficient absent evidence that the work provoked widespread commentary or influenced others in the field. The letters from colleagues did not comment on the impact or influence of her written work. The AAO rejected her argument that contribution to management of 'mega events' is 'in and of itself original work,' finding that while the FIFA World Cup and Olympics are significant events, this alone does not establish that a particular presentation or briefing prepared for one edition is an 'original contribution of major significance.' She did not establish how her manuals, handbooks, and presentations were highly influential or of major significance.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed eligibility based on her work for the Rio 2016 Olympic Games and 2014 FIFA World Cup, asserting that the nature of these events and scope of work assigned to her 'clearly elucidates that the display of [her] work process, design and objectives were ultimately showcased and displayed in the qualities of production and directorship' of these venues. On appeal, she emphasized her responsibility for the conception, planning, development, and execution of programs for the 2014 FIFA World Cup Brazil that hosted prominent world leaders and were 'aimed to showcase the Brazil culture and hospitality,' including regional decor, objects, elements, and traditional local food. She resubmitted a briefing and associated concept development documentation, as well as evidence of the proposal submitted by the Brazilian hospitality services company that was ultimately selected to provide decor and catering.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined the petitioner's primary duties were not related to the display of 'artistic elements,' and that these events were not 'artistic exhibitions or showcases.' The AAO found that while the briefing document mentioned cultural elements (such as 'regional flair,' 'predominant Brazilian decoration,' and 'international cuisine'), it also provided guidelines for non-cultural elements. While the petitioner was involved in selecting the winning bidder, the evidence reflected that the artistic concept for the decor was developed by that bidder, not by the petitioner. Even if the area of a major international sporting event qualified as an 'artistic exhibition or showcase,' the evidence did not establish that she created the work displayed. Since she did not establish that the event was an artistic exhibition or showcase that displayed her own work, she did not meet the criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of her management employment with the local organizing committees for the 2016 Summer Olympic Games and the 2014 FIFA World Cup held in Brazil, along with detailed letters from former supervisors describing her duties and contributions.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director concluded, and the AAO agreed, that the evidence supported this conclusion and sufficiently documented her critical roles as a management employee of the local organizing committees for the 2016 Summer Olympic Games and the 2014 FIFA World Cup held in Brazil. This was the only criterion the petitioner satisfied.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: At the time of filing, the petitioner submitted several employment contracts. In response to the RFE, she submitted copies of her Brazilian annual individual income tax returns for 2015 and 2016. In a statement, she claimed a 'senior event manager' in Brazil earns a median salary of R$7,500 per month or R$97,000 per year, attributing this figure to 'data of Brazilian Geography and Statistic Institute (IBGE),' but did not provide supporting documentation. On appeal, she submitted for the first time a geographical compensation survey for the occupation of event manager in Rio de Janeiro obtained from Glassdoor. She also claimed earnings in 2018 (subsequent to filing).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined the petitioner did not satisfy this criterion because she submitted 'no evidence corroborating' the stated R$97,000 median salary for her occupation and geographic location. The petitioner did not address this deficiency in her motion to reopen/reconsider or initial appeal. The AAO refused to accept the Glassdoor compensation survey submitted for the first time on appeal, citing Matter of Soriano and Matter of Obaigbena, because the petitioner had been put on notice of the deficiency and given an opportunity to respond. The AAO also rejected the 2018 earnings claim because eligibility must be established as of the filing date (May 2017) under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1).

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner satisfied only one of the ten regulatory criteria (leading or critical role) rather than the required minimum of three, and therefore did not meet the initial evidence requirements. Because initial evidence was not satisfied, the AAO stated it did not need to provide a Kazarian final merits determination, but nevertheless reviewed the record in the aggregate and concluded it did not support a finding that the petitioner established the required acclaim and recognition. The AAO emphasized that the EB-1A classification is intended for individuals already at the top of their field, not those progressing toward the top, and that the petitioner had not shown her work was indicative of sustained national or international acclaim or consistent with a 'career of acclaimed work in the field.' Each reason was considered an independent and alternate basis for the decision.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226 (11th Cir. 2005)
United States v. Cunningham, 161 F.3d 1343 (11th Cir. 1998)
Matter of Price, 20 I&N Dec. 953 (Assoc. Comm'r 1994)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Several procedural issues were noted: (1) The petitioner waived the judging and scholarly articles criteria by failing to contest the Director's findings on appeal, per Matter of R-A-M-. (2) Much of her evidence (SMA/WISE memberships approved May 2018, Orlando BrazilUSA Magazine article from 2018, 2018 earnings) post-dated the petition filing in May 2017 and was rejected under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1) requiring eligibility from time of filing. (3) The Glassdoor compensation survey was rejected because it was submitted for the first time on appeal after the petitioner had been put on notice of the evidentiary deficiency in the RFE, per Matter of Soriano and Matter of Obaigbena. (4) This was the second appeal—the AAO had previously withdrawn the Director's original decision and remanded for a new decision, which the Director again denied.
